abstract: 1,3-Disubstituted bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes (BCPs) are high-value motifs in medicinal chemistry, serving as robust, three-dimensional bioisosteres for para-substituted benzene rings. Despite their ability to improve the metabolic stability and solubility of drug candidates, their widespread adoption is hindered by the synthetic challenges associated with [1.1.1]propellane, a volatile and unstable precursor. Here, we introduce a series of stable BCP-thianthrenium reagents that serve as versatile platforms for O-, N-, and C-bicyclo[1.1.1]pentylation. These reagents are non-hygroscopic solids with high thermal stability, facilitating safe storage and handling. Leveraging the unique redox and stereoelectronic properties of the thianthrene scaffold, these salts undergo single-electron reduction to generate BCP radicals via selective mesolytic cleavage of the exocyclic C–S bond. This reactivity enables a range of photoredox-mediated transition-metal-catalyzed transformations, including the first reported synthesis of aryl bicyclo[1.1.1]pentyl ethers and broad-scope C–N and C–C cross-couplings. The method demonstrates exceptional functional group tolerance and is suitable for the late-stage diversification of complex pharmaceuticals, providing a practical and expansive toolkit for incorporating BCP motifs into bioactive molecules.
